front: Poem: describes 'Brute beauty and valour and act, oh, air, pride, plume' and ends with 'blue-bleak embers' that 'gash gold-vermillion'
Poem: praises 'the achieve of, the mastery of the thing!' in a poem about a falcon called 'morning's minion'
Poem: the bird 'rung upon the rein of a wimpling wing / In his ecstasy'; various interpretations center on the word 'Buckle'
Poem: the words 'sheer plod' in the final stanza are marked with diacritics to stress them
Poem: describes 'skies of couple-colour as a brinded cow,' 'rose-moles all in stipple upon trout that swim,' and 'fresh-firecoal chestnut-falls'
Poem: praises 'All things counter, original, spare, strange' and ends 'He fathers-forth whose beauty is past change'; uses the curtal sonnet form
Poem: the ship 'drove in the dark to leeward' and 'struck-not a reef or rock / But the combs of a smother of sand'
back: "The Wreck of the Deutschland" (Gerard Manley Hopkins)

front: Poem: Death declares 'Some find me a sword; some the flange and the rail; flame, fang, or flood'
back: "The Wreck of the Deutschland" (Gerard Manley Hopkins)

front: Poem: ends with the tongue-twisting 'Our hearts' charity's hearth's fire, our thoughts' chivalry's throng's Lord'
back: "The Wreck of the Deutschland" (Gerard Manley Hopkins)

front: Poem: Robert Bridges claimed to have been 'shamefully worsted' by a 'frontal assault' on this 35-stanza poem about five drowned Franciscan nuns
back: "The Wreck of the Deutschland" (Gerard Manley Hopkins)

front: Poem: Elizabeth Bishop was haunted by its 'anvil-ding,' repeating the sound 'Clang' in 'In the Village'
back: "The Wreck of the Deutschland" (Gerard Manley Hopkins)

front: Poem: the editors of a Jesuit magazine 'dared not print' this poem about five Franciscan nuns
back: "The Wreck of the Deutschland" (Gerard Manley Hopkins)

front: Poem: 'Carrion Comfort' closes with 'That night, that year / Of now done darkness I wretch lay wrestling with (my God!) my God'
Poem: 'No Worst, There is None' asks 'Mary, mother of us, where is your relief?' and ends 'all / Life death does end and each day dies with sleep'
Poem: 'To Seem the Stranger Lies My Lot' describes three 'Removes' and laments isolation at a 'third Remove' in Ireland
Poem: this set of six sonnets was described to Robert Bridges as 'the thin gleanings of a long weary while'; never sent to Bridges
Poem: mourns felled trees with 'aspens dear' 'not spared'; creates the verb 'unselve' to demonstrate 'inscape'
Poem: ends 'Rural scene, a rural scene / Sweet especial rural scene'; mentions 'Ten or twelve, only ten or twelve / Strokes of havoc'
Poem: the title attribute 'will flame out, like shining from shook foil' and 'gathers to a greatness, like the ooze of oil'
Poem: observes 'Generations have trod, have trod, have trod; / And all is seared with trade'
Poem: 'Margaret, are you grieving / Over Goldengrove unleaving?'; describes 'worlds of wanwood leafmeal'
Poem: describes a 'Towery city' as 'Cuckoo-echoing, bell-swarmed, lark charmed, rook-racked, river-rounded'; concludes with 'Mary without spot'
Poem: 'each mortal thing does one thing and the same' -- 'What I do is me: for that I came'
Poem: the Leaden Echo ends in 'despair, despair, despair, despair'; the Golden Echo ends 'Yonder, yes yonder, yonder, / Yonder'; asks 'how to keep' beauty from 'vanishing away'
back: "The Leaden Echo and the Golden Echo" (Gerard Manley Hopkins)

front: Poem: describes a 'heart's clarion' and 'world's wildfire'; states 'this... poor potsherd, patch, matchwood... is Immortal Diamond'
back: "That Nature is a Heraclitean Fire" (Gerard Manley Hopkins)

front: Poem: uses the Welsh verse form cynghanedd; 'Left hand, off land, I hear the lark ascend'
back: "The Sea and the Skylark" (Gerard Manley Hopkins)

front: Poet: concept of 'inscape' (essential uniqueness of a thing) and 'instress' derived from reading Duns Scotus
Poet: poems led off the influential Faber Book of Modern Verse despite being long dead
Poet: last words were reportedly 'I am so happy, I am so happy. I loved my life' despite late-life depression
